title: Zero-Sum Game (Win or Lose)
the_cheat_code: Think of a zero-sum game like a fixed pie: if you get a bigger slice, others must get smaller. One person’s win is exactly another’s loss.
the_casino_trap: Imagine thinking that your success must make someone else fail. You get a top score, so your friend thinks they scored less because of you. This misunderstanding can lead to jealousy or sabotage.
the_real_world_hack: You and a friend split a ₹100 prize from a quiz. Instead of fighting, you agree each gets ₹50. Both of you earn something. You see that sharing can benefit both, not just beating each other.
the_superpower: You learn to spot situations where a win-win is possible. You avoid needless fights and make deals where everyone wins more.
the_mission: Next time you and a friend both want something, try to find a way to divide it fairly so both of you benefit.

title: Positive-Sum Game (Win-Win Situation)
the_cheat_code: In a positive-sum game, the pie can grow. If you cooperate or share, everyone can get more. Working together creates more rewards than fighting over fixed shares.
the_casino_trap: Some people think helping you means hurting me. For example, they keep all exam tips secret fearing others get smarter. This mindset prevents win-win deals.
the_real_world_hack: You and a friend start a side business selling snacks. By cooperating, you sell more than each selling alone. Both of you make extra money. Both win more than if you competed.
the_superpower: You spot when cooperation will boost everyone's success. You become known as someone who creates win-win deals instead of fights.
the_mission: Next time you face a conflict, suggest a solution where everyone wins a bit, like sharing study notes or splitting chores.

title: Prisoner's Dilemma (Teamwork or Betrayal?)
the_cheat_code: Prisoner's Dilemma is a game where two friends can either cooperate or betray. If both cooperate, each gets a moderate reward. If one betrays and the other cooperates, the betrayer wins big and the cooperator gets little. If both betray, both get small punishment.
the_casino_trap: It feels safer to betray just in case the other cheats. But if both players think this and betray, they both lose out more than if they had cooperated.
the_real_world_hack: Imagine you and a classmate must confess for cheating together. If both stay silent, both of you just get a warning and keep studying. But if one lies and betrays the other, the liar goes free and the other gets in trouble.
the_superpower: You learn to recognize when cooperation helps both people, even if betraying looks tempting. You avoid traps where everyone loses because they didn't trust each other.
the_mission: Think of a situation with a friend where you both could either help or hurt each other, and decide what happens in each case.

title: Tit-for-Tat (The Fair Trade Strategy)
the_cheat_code: Tit-for-Tat means start by cooperating, then mirror your partner's last move. If they help you, help them next. If they harm you, you harm them back.
the_casino_trap: If you start by betraying or always betray, your friends will too and no one wins in the long run. A mean start builds distrust.
the_real_world_hack: You and your sibling are cleaning the house together. You help first, and next round your sibling helps you. After some time, you split cleaning chores fairly and even get ice cream reward.
the_superpower: You become the kind of person others trust and want to work with. When they see you cooperate, they cooperate, creating a cycle of fairness.
the_mission: Help someone once without any gain. If they return the favor, keep helping; if not, be careful next time.

title: First-Order Thinking (Short-Term Focus)
the_cheat_code: First-order thinking means only looking at the immediate result. You pick what seems best right now, without thinking about later consequences.
the_casino_trap: People trick themselves by focusing on instant pleasure. For example, skipping exam study to play a video game sounds fun now, but leads to failure later.
the_real_world_hack: Imagine your friends invite you to party the night before exam. First-order thinking says 'fun now!' but second-order thinking warns of flunking.
the_superpower: You catch yourself before making a short-sighted choice. You think long-term, like a chess player planning moves ahead.
the_mission: Next time you make a quick decision, pause and ask: 'What happens tomorrow?'

title: Second-Order Thinking (Thinking Two Moves Ahead)
the_cheat_code: Second-order thinking means you predict not just the result of your choice, but what happens after that. It’s like playing chess, planning several moves ahead.
the_casino_trap: If you only react without predicting, others can outsmart you. For instance, you think failing a test is just one bad grade, but one step ahead you see it affects college plans.
the_real_world_hack: Your friend lies to you. Before you call them out, think: How might they react? If you react angrily now, they might never talk. Planning two steps ahead lets you stay calm and handle it better.
the_superpower: You spot traps and long-term effects before anyone else. You avoid problems that surprise others later.
the_mission: When deciding something, ask: 'If I do this, what will happen next?' then one more step after that.

title: Incentives (Why People Do Things)
the_cheat_code: Incentives are rewards or punishments that make us act. People usually do something if they get more good things than bad. Understanding them helps predict choices.
the_casino_trap: If you ignore what someone wants, you get fooled. For example, you think your brother studies out of love for math, but he actually wants your parents’ praise (points for good grades).
the_real_world_hack: Your coach promises bonus practice time for good performance. You work harder because of that extra playtime. You spot that the promise (incentive) made you train.
the_superpower: You can motivate others or yourself by using incentives. You become a leader who knows how to make people do the right thing.
the_mission: Find out why someone works hard or not. Then try using a small reward or penalty to change an outcome.

title: Coordination (Teamwork Wins)
the_cheat_code: Coordination means working together so everyone’s actions match. When people coordinate, they all do better, like picking a meeting time everyone can make.
the_casino_trap: If people fail to coordinate, they miss out. Imagine two classmates doing the same homework without talking: they do duplicate work and waste time.
the_real_world_hack: You and friends plan to meet at 5 PM for a movie. If everyone agrees and comes together, you all watch the movie happily. That’s coordination.
the_superpower: You become great at arranging group plans or projects. Your team always succeeds because you make sure everyone does their part.
the_mission: Try planning an activity with a friend. Make sure both know what to do and when. See how smooth it goes.

title: Nash Equilibrium (Stable Situation)
the_cheat_code: A Nash equilibrium is a situation where no one can improve by changing strategy alone. Everyone’s choice matches up so nobody wants to switch by themselves.
the_casino_trap: People sometimes stick with a bad plan because nobody will change if they do. For example, if everyone cheats in class, you think 'even if I study honestly, I won’t beat cheaters so why bother?'
the_real_world_hack: On a group project, everyone agreed to share tasks equally. No one wants to change the plan alone, because any change would hurt their own grade. That stable plan is a Nash equilibrium.
the_superpower: You spot stable deals and also break bad ones. You see when changing strategy won’t help unless others change too.
the_mission: Think of a situation where people keep doing the same thing. Ask: 'If I change my mind, will it help?' If not, it might be a stable situation.

title: Dominant Strategy (Always the Best Move)
the_cheat_code: A dominant strategy is a choice that gives the best outcome no matter what others do. It’s like always choosing your strongest card in a game.
the_casino_trap: If you don’t see it, you might keep switching actions for no reason. If you do have it, sometimes people predict you.
the_real_world_hack: Studying could be your dominant strategy: it helps your score no matter what other students do. You always choose to study.
the_superpower: You discover and use strategies that win in any situation. You play your best move every time.
the_mission: Think of a choice where one option is clearly always best. Will you take it every time?

title: Trust & Reputation (Your Social Credit)
the_cheat_code: Your reputation is like a score: being fair and trustworthy means people will trust and cooperate with you. If you break trust, people avoid you.
the_casino_trap: If you betray friends often, no one will trust you, so you’ll never make deals. Bad reputation closes doors.
the_real_world_hack: You borrow a book and return it on time, so the owner is happy and lends more. Your good reputation brings rewards.
the_superpower: You build goodwill. People help you, give second chances, and trust you with big projects or secrets.
the_mission: Help someone without asking for anything. Observe how it affects their view of you.

title: Cooperation vs Competition
the_cheat_code: Cooperation means working together to win. Competition means trying to beat others. Sometimes working with others gets a better result than fighting for yourself.
the_casino_trap: Thinking you must always compete can hurt you. If everyone competes alone, few succeed. Team effort can give higher rewards for all.
the_real_world_hack: In a relay race, runners hand the baton to help win. If each ran only for personal glory, the team loses. By cooperating, everyone wins a medal.
the_superpower: You know when to team up or go solo. You switch modes and get the best outcome by picking the right approach.
the_mission: Choose a project where you can work with a classmate. Notice how cooperating changes the result.

title: Scarcity & Choice (Limited Resources)
the_cheat_code: When resources (time, money, things) are limited, you must choose carefully. Scarcity means there isn’t enough for everyone to get everything they want.
the_casino_trap: If you forget scarcity, you might try to do everything and fail. People sometimes act like they have unlimited time or money.
the_real_world_hack: You have only ₹50 for snacks. You choose either a large samosa or a soda. You can’t have both, so you decide based on which you want more.
the_superpower: You prioritize what matters and make smart decisions knowing you can’t have it all.
the_mission: Think of something you want most today (like playing a game or eating a treat). Remember you can’t do everything. Plan your choice.

title: Negotiation (Win-Win Deal Making)
the_cheat_code: Negotiation means talking to find a deal that both sides like. Good negotiators look for win-win, not trick others. It’s like haggling in a market to find a fair price.
the_casino_trap: Some try to force one-sided deals, which leads to fights. For example, a sibling demands chores without reward, then the other refuses. No agreement happens.
the_real_world_hack: You want extra playtime. You promise to help with groceries. Your parents agree. You both win: you play more, they get help.
the_superpower: You can make any deal better. You find solutions where both people walk away happy.
the_mission: Negotiate with a friend or parent on something small. Try to find a solution where both of you gain.

title: Repeated Interactions (Long-Term Game)
the_cheat_code: When you deal with someone again and again, you care about future too. You can’t just cheat once, because you’ll meet again. Long-term thinking changes how you play.
the_casino_trap: Treating one-time deals as long-term can be bad. For example, if you steal candy from a friend now, you might not trust them next time. Patterns repeat.
the_real_world_hack: Your tutor remembers if you are honest each session. You keep doing homework honestly so they keep helping you. You play this long game.
the_superpower: You make friends and partners by being good repeatedly. People trust you and want to work with you in the future.
the_mission: Think of someone you see often, like a teacher. Next time, do something that makes them happy to work with you again.

title: Decision Tree (Plan the Steps Ahead)
the_cheat_code: A decision tree is like drawing out your choices and what might happen after each. You map steps and results to choose the best path.
the_casino_trap: Without a plan, you might randomly guess and feel confused. People forget to map consequences and waste time figuring out what to do.
the_real_world_hack: Before taking a new course, you write down: if I pass, then next step is this; if I fail, another step is that. This map helps decide what to do next.
the_superpower: You make clear plans even when situations are complex. You never get surprised because you saw most outcomes beforehand.
the_mission: For a simple choice (like studying vs playing), sketch what could happen with each choice on paper.

title: Minimax Strategy (Safest Move)
the_cheat_code: Minimax means picking the move that maximizes your minimum gain. You focus on the worst possible outcome and make it as good as possible.
the_casino_trap: If you ignore worst-case scenarios, you might make risky decisions. For example, you might bet all your pocket money on one number and lose everything.
the_real_world_hack: You prepare backup notes for each subject. Even if you mess up one exam, you still can pass because of your safety net.
the_superpower: You know how to protect yourself from disasters. You make choices that keep you safe even in the worst case.
the_mission: Next time, think 'If everything goes wrong, what can I do to still be okay?'

title: Free Rider Problem (Taking Without Giving)
the_cheat_code: Free rider problem is when someone benefits from group work without contributing. It hurts groups because some do all work while others enjoy results for free.
the_casino_trap: You might think 'I’ll let others do it'. But if everyone thinks that, projects fail because nobody helps.
the_real_world_hack: In a group study, one friend doesn’t prepare but still passes by copying others. Everyone works hard, and one person gets the benefit without effort.
the_superpower: You make sure you contribute, and you know when to address freeloaders. You keep group work fair.
the_mission: Next group project, do your fair share. Notice if others do too.

title: Tragedy of the Commons (When Everyone Takes)
the_cheat_code: Tragedy of the Commons is when individuals use a shared resource selfishly, causing it to deplete and harming everyone.
the_casino_trap: You might think 'I can use more because I’m just one person'. But if everyone thinks that, the resource runs out.
the_real_world_hack: Sharing a cricket pitch: if one person hogs time and practice, others get less. If everyone hogs, the pitch gets ruined.
the_superpower: You keep community resources safe. You either share fairly or set rules so everyone benefits.
the_mission: Next time you use a shared resource (like a computer or field), use only your fair share and see if it helps others too.

title: Expected Value (The Gambler's Math)
the_cheat_code: Expected Value (EV) is a math trick to check if a gamble is worth it. You multiply each prize by its chance, add them up, then subtract the cost. If positive, it's a good bet in the long run.
the_casino_trap: Buying a lottery ticket seems fun because the prize is huge. But the chance is tiny. For example, a ₹100 ticket with 1 in 1,000,000 chance to win ₹1 Crore has EV just ₹0.10. You lose ₹99.90 on average.
the_real_world_hack: You spend 30 minutes playing a game instead of studying. The 'prize' is fun time, but the 'cost' is risking your exam grade. The expected value of skipping study is negative, so you say no and study instead.
the_superpower: You become like a poker champion: only take risks that math says are good deals, not just because they feel exciting.
the_mission: Pick a choice (like skipping homework for fun). Write down the best and worst things that happen. Estimate how likely each is. Compare benefits vs costs.

title: Base Rate (Start with the Defaults)
the_cheat_code: Base rate is the overall frequency of something. Always check the general odds first. For example, if 90% of students pass an exam, start with the idea that a random student likely passes.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring base rates can fool you. If one student got 100/100, you might think everyone will ace it. But if only 10% normally score full, odds say most won’t.
the_real_world_hack: Your school has only 5% seats on the sports team. Even if you are good, remember most tryouts fail. Don’t assume you’ll make it just because you like the sport.
the_superpower: You judge chances correctly by remembering the averages. You won’t be misled by rare events.
the_mission: Find a statistic about something you care about (like pass rates) and use it next time to set real expectations.

title: Law of Large Numbers (Big Samples Rule)
the_cheat_code: The Law of Large Numbers says that as you repeat an experiment many times, the average result gets closer to the true probability. More trials means more reliable results.
the_casino_trap: People fall for short streaks. E.g., a coin flipped 10 times might show 8 heads, but that’s luck. They incorrectly think the coin is biased.
the_real_world_hack: A student rolls a dice 6 times and gets many sixes, thinking it’s lucky. Another rolls 100 times and sees about 1/6 sixes. The second trusts long-run averages.
the_superpower: You trust math of big numbers, so you don’t make hasty decisions based on a few tries. You know bigger data is more accurate.
the_mission: Flip a coin 20 times and see how close you get to 50% heads. The more flips, the closer you’ll get.

title: Luck vs Skill (Chance vs Practice)
the_cheat_code: Luck vs Skill means figuring out what we can control and what we can’t. In games or life, some outcomes are pure chance, others come from practice and effort.
the_casino_trap: Confusing luck and skill fools people. A beginner wins the first match and thinks it’s talent. Or a pro loses one game and feels useless. Both misjudge random swings.
the_real_world_hack: You ace a test without studying, but know it was luck, not skill. Next time you study to get real skill. You give credit to preparation or pure chance correctly.
the_superpower: You focus on improving what you can (skill) and accept random results as just chance, not personal failure.
the_mission: Think of one success of yours. Ask yourself: did luck play a part or was it your hard work?

title: Variance (Ups and Downs)
the_cheat_code: Variance measures how spread out outcomes can be. High variance means results can swing big, low variance means results stay close to average.
the_casino_trap: Seeing one high or low and panicking. One game’s big win doesn’t mean every game will be the same.
the_real_world_hack: You score 60, 90, 70 on three tests. It’s normal variance. You know one score isn’t destiny; over many tests you’ll see your average.
the_superpower: You ride out ups and downs. You know one bad day doesn’t ruin everything.
the_mission: Look at your last 5 test scores and calculate your average. Notice how the scores differ from that average.

title: Independent Events (Don’t Affect Each Other)
the_cheat_code: Independent events are unrelated: the result of one doesn’t change the next. Like flipping a coin twice: first flip doesn’t affect second flip.
the_casino_trap: We think events are connected when they’re not. Eg: after five heads in a row, many think ‘tails is due’. This is the gambler’s fallacy.
the_real_world_hack: Your exam was easy last time, but you study just as hard next time, because each test is independent of the last.
the_superpower: You make decisions based on actual odds, not mistaken patterns. You don’t let past independent events fool you.
the_mission: Flip a coin twice. Notice that the chance of heads on the second flip is still 50%, no matter what happened first.

title: Probability (The Math of Chance)
the_cheat_code: Probability is a number between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%) that tells how likely something is. 1 means sure, 0 means impossible.
the_casino_trap: Many mix up chance and outcome. They might say 50% on a quiz means they will definitely get half right. But 50% just means on average, half the time.
the_real_world_hack: You check the weather: 70% chance of rain means rain is likely but not certain. You take an umbrella because it might rain.
the_superpower: You judge risks correctly: whether something is likely or not. You make smart choices using probabilities (like carrying umbrella if rain chance is high).
the_mission: Think of something with chance (like drawing a colored marble). Write its probability and see if your expectation matches the result after trying.

title: Conditional Probability (Chances with Clues)
the_cheat_code: Conditional probability is the chance of something happening when you have extra information. For example, knowing today is Monday might change the chance it will rain later.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring given info can mislead you. Eg: assuming any card draw has 1/4 chance of being hearts, even if someone already showed a heart.
the_real_world_hack: You have a 1% chance to win a random gift, but if you have a special coupon, it jumps to 50%. Knowing you have the coupon changes the odds.
the_superpower: You update probabilities when you get new clues. You make sharper predictions using all info you have.
the_mission: Imagine there are 100 balls, 50 red, 50 blue. If you know one random ball drawn is red, what’s the chance the next is red? Think how info changed the odds.

title: Regression to the Mean (Back to Normal)
the_cheat_code: Extreme results tend to be followed by more normal ones. If you do very well or poorly once, next time you’re likely closer to your usual average.
the_casino_trap: After a big win, people might gamble thinking they’re ‘hot’. But luck usually balances out, so they often lose.
the_real_world_hack: You score very low on a test. Because you usually do better, next test you score closer to your normal average, not as low.
the_superpower: You don’t overreact to one big result. You expect things will even out over time.
the_mission: Think of one big success or failure of yours. How did your next attempt turn out? Notice the difference.

title: Compound Probability (Chain of Chances)
the_cheat_code: To find the chance of multiple independent events happening, multiply their probabilities. For example, flipping heads then heads (each 1/2) gives 1/2 * 1/2 = 1/4.
the_casino_trap: Not multiplying, people often guess wrong. They might think two 50% chances are still 50%, but the math is 25%.
the_real_world_hack: You flip a coin and roll a die. Chance of heads AND a 6 is 1/2 * 1/6 = 1/12. You calculate combined chances.
the_superpower: You calculate chances of multi-step events correctly. You won’t be fooled by games with several steps.
the_mission: Flip a coin and roll a die. Calculate the chance of getting heads and a 6. Then try it to check your math.

title: Bayes' Rule (Smart Updates)
the_cheat_code: Bayes' Rule shows how to update probabilities with new evidence. If something was unlikely, but you see a clue, you recalculate its chance.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring evidence or being fooled by test accuracy. People often forget that rare things can still be unlikely even if test is positive.
the_real_world_hack: A disease test says 99% accurate. If only 1% of people have the disease, then even a positive test gives about 50% chance. You see how to update with base rate.
the_superpower: You combine old and new info to see the real picture. You don’t get tricked by raw numbers without context.
the_mission: If 1 out of 1000 students is a champion and you hear someone won a contest, think: what’s the chance they’re the champion? Update using new info.

title: Exponential Growth (Double Quick!)
the_cheat_code: Exponential growth means something grows faster and faster. Like doubling each step: 1 becomes 2, then 4, 8, 16... It becomes huge quickly.
the_casino_trap: We think linearly. A small 10% yearly growth sounds small, but over many years it becomes huge. People underestimate compounding.
the_real_world_hack: You save ₹100 in a bank with 10% interest. After 7 years, it’s nearly ₹200 because of compounding interest.
the_superpower: You use compound benefits (like saving money or practicing skills steadily) to get big results later.
the_mission: Try folding paper: each fold doubles thickness. Fold a few times and see how thick it gets (if you could!).

title: Time Value of Money (Money Grows Over Time)
the_cheat_code: A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow, because you can invest it. Money grows over time with interest or opportunities.
the_casino_trap: Procrastinating on savings or investments. Some think ₹100 next year is as good as ₹100 today, but inflation or interest changes value.
the_real_world_hack: Your parents give you ₹500 now instead of next month. You invest it, and one year later it’s ₹550. You see why earlier is better.
the_superpower: You know to use money or time early. You benefit from any growth instead of losing value to inflation.
the_mission: If someone offers you ₹100 now or ₹110 in a month, choose ₹100 now. Save or invest it to get more.

title: Fermi Estimate (Quick Guess Math)
the_cheat_code: Fermi estimation breaks big unknowns into smaller guesses. Estimate each part roughly and multiply/add. It’s like solving hard questions with easy math.
the_casino_trap: We often say “I have no idea” without trying to guess. Missing a quick, useful estimate leaves us stuck.
the_real_world_hack: Estimating how many students in school: count one classroom and multiply by number of classes. You get a good ballpark without counting everyone.
the_superpower: You confidently answer tough questions by breaking them down. You can estimate populations, costs, distances with simple math.
the_mission: Estimate something: how many books are in your city’s library? Break it into shelves × books per shelf.

title: Asymmetric Risk (Big Upside, Small Downside)
the_cheat_code: Asymmetric risk is when the worst you lose is small but the best gain is huge. Think lottery or startup: you risk little, but could win big.
the_casino_trap: Fear of small loss stops big wins. People avoid small bets with big prizes, ignoring huge upside. They choose little gain with big safety.
the_real_world_hack: You buy a ₹10 lottery ticket. You can only lose ₹10, but you could win ₹1 Crore. Big upside for little cost.
the_superpower: You spot deals with high reward vs low cost. You take small risks that others avoid and often win big.
the_mission: Find a contest or bet where the entry fee is low but prize is high. Even if chance is small, it’s worth the shot.

title: Percentage vs Actual (Real vs Relative)
the_cheat_code: Percentages show relative size. To understand deals, convert them to real numbers. For example, 50% of 10 is 5 rupees; 50% of 100 is 50 rupees.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring the base number. If two shops say "50% off", one on ₹100 item and one on ₹1000 item, the real savings (50 vs 500 rupees) differ a lot.
the_real_world_hack: A ₹200 shirt is 50% off (₹100). A ₹2000 phone is 50% off (₹1000). The big deal is on the phone, even though both say 50%.
the_superpower: You get the true value of deals and stats. You won’t be fooled by big percentages hiding small bases.
the_mission: See a discount or stat, and convert it to actual numbers to really understand it.

title: Opportunity Cost (What You Give Up)
the_cheat_code: Opportunity cost is what you miss by choosing something else. Every choice has a hidden cost of the next best thing you didn’t do.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring the real cost of time or money. Students skip studying for games, forgetting the future benefit (passing exam) they lose.
the_real_world_hack: If you spend ₹100 on candy, your opportunity cost is not buying a book you wanted. You lost that benefit.
the_superpower: You make smarter choices by considering what you're missing. You pick options with the best overall benefit.
the_mission: Think of a decision: list what you lose by picking one option over another (like playing a game vs studying).

title: Diversification (Don't Put All Eggs in One Basket)
the_cheat_code: Diversification spreads risk. By having many smaller bets or plans instead of one, you protect from total loss. If one fails, others might succeed.
the_casino_trap: Risking everything on one bet. Students do this by focusing only on one idea for career, ignoring other options.
the_real_world_hack: You apply to 3 college courses instead of just 1. Even if one application fails, others might succeed.
the_superpower: You reduce risk by balancing choices. Even if one plan fails, others support you.
the_mission: Find one goal and plan two different ways to achieve it, so if one path fails, you have another.

title: Budgeting (Plan Your Spending)
the_cheat_code: Budgeting means planning how to use your money over time so you don’t run out. Track income and expenses and save for goals.
the_casino_trap: Spending impulsively until money is gone. Many students waste pocket money and have none left for needs.
the_real_world_hack: You allocate ₹200 per week for snacks and ₹100 for savings. By tracking, you always have money for important needs.
the_superpower: You know exactly where your money goes. You save for big things easily and avoid debt.
the_mission: Make a simple budget for your next pocket money: list essentials, fun money, and savings.

title: Ratios & Proportions (Fair Shares)
the_cheat_code: Ratios compare parts. If 3 friends share 12 sweets in ratio 1:2:3, they get 2, 4, and 6 sweets respectively. Proportions keep things fair.
the_casino_trap: Misunderstanding equal division. You might split tasks or money unevenly without realizing who does more.
the_real_world_hack: Three siblings decide chores in ratio 1:1:2. First two do 1 chore each, third does 2. It’s fair by agreement.
the_superpower: You solve sharing problems quickly. You split bills, candies, or tasks correctly for everyone.
the_mission: If 4 friends buy a pizza and split the bill in ratio 1:1:2:2, calculate each share.

title: Inflation (Prices Rising)
the_cheat_code: Inflation means prices go up over time. ₹100 today buys more than ₹100 next year, because things get more expensive.
the_casino_trap: Ignoring inflation, you might think ₹100 saved is always the same. Actually, if inflation is 5%, your money buys less after a year.
the_real_world_hack: A movie ticket cost ₹80 five years ago, but now it costs ₹120. You see how saving without interest lost purchasing power.
the_superpower: You plan for the real cost of things. You save or invest more, knowing what money will buy later.
the_mission: Check the price of something (like a samosa) from a few years ago and now. Notice how inflation changed it.

title: Sunk Cost Fallacy (Stop Chasing Losses)
the_cheat_code: Sunk costs are past investments that can't be recovered. Don't let them force you to keep going. Only future gains matter.
the_casino_trap: We say "Already spent ₹500 on this book, so I must finish it" even if it's boring. Past cost shouldn't decide future actions.
the_real_world_hack: You paid for a movie ticket and it’s bad. Instead of sitting through, you leave, saving time even though money is already gone.
the_superpower: You cut losses. When something isn’t working, you move on instead of wasting more time or money.
the_mission: Think of something you invested time in but stopped. Next time, ask "If this were free, would I still continue?" If not, stop.

title: Outcome Bias (Judge Plans, Not Results)
the_cheat_code: Outcome bias is judging decisions by results. A good plan can lead to a bad result by chance, and a bad plan can get lucky. Focus on the plan itself.
the_casino_trap: You might praise a gambler for winning or blame for losing, ignoring strategy. People say "I knew it" without realizing luck was involved.
the_real_world_hack: You flip a coin to decide something and it works out. You think the coin is magic. But the result was luck, not a good decision.
the_superpower: You evaluate actions by reasoning, not only outcome. You learn from decisions whether they succeed or fail.
the_mission: Take a decision that had a good or bad result. Ask: was it because of luck or a strong plan?

title: Survivorship Bias (Only Hear Winners)
the_cheat_code: Survivorship bias means noticing only successes and ignoring failures. Hearing just winners skews your view of how likely success really is.
the_casino_trap: You hear only success stories (like "millionaire dropout"). You think it’s common and forget all who failed.
the_real_world_hack: You read about a dropout who became rich. But many dropouts stay poor. You remember failures too, not just the fame.
the_superpower: You consider failures as well as successes. You make realistic plans knowing success is hard to achieve.
the_mission: Pick a success story you admire. Find someone who tried the same path but didn’t succeed.

title: Gambler's Fallacy (Past Doesn’t Change Odds)
the_cheat_code: Gambler’s fallacy is believing that a random event is ‘due’ after a streak. But in fair games (like coin flips), past results don’t affect the next chance.
the_casino_trap: After flipping heads 5 times, you think "tail is due". This is wrong for independent events. You might bet on the wrong outcome.
the_real_world_hack: On a cricket coin toss, if two tails came up, people might say "heads must come next". But it’s still 50-50 each toss.
the_superpower: You trust math, not superstitions. You know each event is independent, so you make better choices.
the_mission: Flip a coin 10 times. Notice each flip is 50% heads, regardless of previous flips.

title: Strawman Fallacy (Don’t Attack a Fake Argument)
the_cheat_code: Strawman fallacy is misrepresenting someone’s argument to make it easier to attack. You argue against a weaker version instead of the real point.
the_casino_trap: Instead of addressing the real point, you argue against something not said. This avoids real issues.
the_real_world_hack: Friend says "I want to study more." You reply "So you never want to have fun again!" That’s a strawman argument.
the_superpower: You fight the real issue, not a fake one. You win honest debates and understand others better.
the_mission: When someone makes a claim, repeat it in your own words first. This stops you from accidentally creating a strawman.

title: Anchoring (First Number Tricks You)
the_cheat_code: Anchoring means relying too much on the first number or info you hear. That first ‘anchor’ stays in your mind and biases your decisions.
the_casino_trap: If first salary offered is ₹50000, you adjust your expectations around it, even if you could get more.
the_real_world_hack: At a market, a shopkeeper first says ₹500 for an item. You bargain from there, even if fair price was ₹200.
the_superpower: You question initial offers or info. You negotiate better by setting your own reference points.
the_mission: Next time you hear a price or statistic, ask yourself if it’s just a starting point or the real value.

title: Confirmation Bias (Seeing Only What You Believe)
the_cheat_code: Confirmation bias means noticing only facts that match what you already believe and ignoring the rest. You end up only finding proof for your own ideas.
the_casino_trap: If you only watch news that agrees with you, you never learn other perspectives and stay wrong.
the_real_world_hack: You think your friend is bad at sports, so you only remember the times they missed a shot, forgetting all the times they scored.
the_superpower: You search for evidence on both sides. You become a balanced thinker and make fair judgments.
the_mission: Take one belief you have. Find one fact that goes against it. Force yourself to consider it.

title: Availability Bias (Loud Events Seem Common)
the_cheat_code: Availability bias is thinking events are more common because they are easier to recall. If something is big or reported a lot, you assume it happens often.
the_casino_trap: You watch news of plane crashes and think flying is dangerous, ignoring that car accidents are much more frequent.
the_real_world_hack: You hear about a student winning a contest. You think prizes are easy, forgetting that most participants win nothing.
the_superpower: You check actual data, not just headlines. You make decisions on facts, not just scary stories.
the_mission: Name something scary (like a shark attack). Look up how often it happens each year compared to something safe (like bike accidents).

title: Recency Bias (Latest Events Are Biggest)
the_cheat_code: Recency bias means giving too much importance to recent events. The latest thing you saw or did seems the biggest deal.
the_casino_trap: You judge performance by the last test only. Getting low marks recently makes you think you’ll fail always, ignoring past successes.
the_real_world_hack: Your team lost its last two matches, so you think they’re a bad team now, even though they won most games before.
the_superpower: You keep perspective by considering more history. You don’t panic over one bad event.
the_mission: When you see a recent story, ask yourself: 'What happened earlier this year on the same topic?'

title: Hindsight Bias (I Knew It All Along)
the_cheat_code: Hindsight bias is thinking you predicted something after it happened. You say 'I knew it!' even if you didn’t.
the_casino_trap: You feel overconfident after an event and don't learn from mistakes, because you think the outcome was obvious.
the_real_world_hack: After the cricket match, you say 'I knew they would win,' even though before the match you weren't sure. Recognizing this bias helps you prepare better next time.
the_superpower: You remember your actual predictions. This helps you learn and not be fooled by past events.
the_mission: Next time, write down your prediction before an event. After it happens, compare without changing your answer to 'I knew it.'

title: Bandwagon Effect (Jumping on Trends)
the_cheat_code: Bandwagon effect is doing something just because others are. You follow the crowd without checking if it’s good for you.
the_casino_trap: Peer pressure leads to bad choices. Just because everyone says a movie is great doesn’t mean you’ll like it. You might waste time and money.
the_real_world_hack: All kids in class buy the newest smartphone. You buy it too, but soon regret it because you didn’t really need it.
the_superpower: You think for yourself and only follow trends you truly like. You avoid unnecessary risks or expenses from peer pressure.
the_mission: Think of a trend you follow. Ask: 'Do I really want this, or just because others do?'

title: Overconfidence Bias (Stay Humble)
the_cheat_code: Overconfidence is thinking you're more likely to succeed than reality. You believe too much in your own ability.
the_casino_trap: You ignore risk. Thinking 'I’ll surely win this bet' can lead to big losses if you’re wrong.
the_real_world_hack: You refuse help on homework saying 'I got this.' Next day you struggle. Overconfidence hurt your grades.
the_superpower: You stay grounded. You study enough and double-check plans, making you more reliable.
the_mission: Before a test, predict your score. Compare with actual. See if you were too high or too low.

title: Appeal to Authority (Verify Facts)
the_cheat_code: Just because someone famous or an 'expert' says something doesn’t make it true. Check the facts, even from authorities.
the_casino_trap: Trusting blindly can mislead you. A celebrity or official might recommend a product, but that doesn’t mean it works.
the_real_world_hack: A coach tells you to spend pocket money on supplements, but you research and see they’re useless without exercise. You didn’t just trust their word.
the_superpower: You always seek evidence. You don’t get fooled by “everyone knows” and you become well-informed.
the_mission: Question a fact from a popular figure. Research it yourself instead of accepting it blindly.

title: False Dilemma (More Than Two Choices)
the_cheat_code: False dilemma is thinking only two outcomes exist. There may be many options besides 'either/or'.
the_casino_trap: You say "Either I pass or I fail this class" and ignore solutions like asking for help. You might think only two extremes exist.
the_real_world_hack: Someone says, "Either you study science or you’re a failure." You know you could also choose arts or commerce. Life isn’t just those two paths.
the_superpower: You look for creative solutions. You find options others miss.
the_mission: Find a problem with an "either/or" claim. Come up with a third choice.

title: Appeal to Ignorance (No Proof ≠ Truth)
the_cheat_code: Just because we lack proof of something doesn’t prove it. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
the_casino_trap: Believing wild claims without proof, like "Ghosts exist because no one proved they don’t." You’re using lack of evidence as proof.
the_real_world_hack: If someone says "Aliens exist because you can’t prove they don’t," you know that’s not valid. You ask for actual evidence.
the_superpower: You demand evidence before believing something. You don’t accept claims just because they’re not disproven.
the_mission: Think of a claim you heard. Ask: "Is there actual proof? Or is it just not disproven?"

title: Cherry Picking (Selective Evidence)
the_cheat_code: Cherry picking is choosing only the evidence that supports your view and ignoring the rest. It’s like showing only your best scores.
the_casino_trap: You brag "I never failed any test!" but ignore the times you did fail. You pick only the positives.
the_real_world_hack: You only remember the times a friend said something nice, forgetting all the times they were mean.
the_superpower: You look at all the data, not just highlights. You spot when someone only shows the good parts.
the_mission: When someone makes a claim, ask for all the evidence, not just the good examples.

title: No Free Lunch (Nothing is Truly Free)
the_cheat_code: No free lunch means you can’t get something for nothing. Everything has a cost, even if hidden.
the_casino_trap: You see 'free' offers and ignore the catch. A free app might sell your data instead.
the_real_world_hack: A friend says "Join my event for free, it’s fun!" Then you find out they want your personal info. It wasn’t free after all.
the_superpower: You always check what the real cost is. If something seems too good, you ask what you’re really paying.
the_mission: Next time something is advertised as free, ask: "What am I really giving in return?"

title: Correlation vs Causation (Coincidence or Cause?)
the_cheat_code: Just because two things happen together doesn’t mean one causes the other. They may just coincide or be caused by something else.
the_casino_trap: You jump to conclusions. Eg: you see many students with new phones got A’s, so you think new phone made them smart, ignoring studying.
the_real_world_hack: When ice cream sales and sunburn cases rise together, it’s summer causing both, not ice cream causing sunburn.
the_superpower: You find the real reasons behind patterns. You avoid blaming the wrong thing for an outcome.
the_mission: Look at two events that happen together. Ask: 'Does one really cause the other, or is it coincidence?'
